content:
Green CI is not a safety system when the tests were also generated.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

It started as a demo, which is how the expensive mistakes often start.
We wired an agent to open pull requests, wait for CI, and merge automatically when green. The audience was internal only. The feature flag was on for eleven minutes in production before someone noticed duplicate subscription rows in the billing table.
Rollback did not take eleven minutes. Rollback took three days because the migration had already run forward, the down path was untested, and the generated tests had never asserted uniqueness under concurrent writes.
Green CI had been the safety argument. Green CI was also generated by the same session that introduced the race.
The Safety Theater of Generated Tests
Tests are only as honest as their oracle. When implementation and tests share the same blind spot, CI becomes a performance of safety.
Our agent added:
→ Happy path coverage for single-user checkout → Mocked payment provider always returning success → Migration adding a column without a unique constraint where we needed one → A feature flag defaulting to on in production because the brief said “enable for testing”
Each piece looked reasonable alone. Together they let two webhook retries create two active subscriptions for one customer.
The tests passed because they never simulated retry timing. They passed loudly, which is worse than failing quietly.
I probably should have blocked auto-merge the moment I saw test files created in the same generation pass as the migration. Independence was theoretical.
Eleven Minutes Is Long Enough
People underestimate how much damage short windows allow when traffic is non-trivial and writes are sticky.
Eleven minutes at our scale was thousands of checkout attempts and dozens of duplicated entitlements. Support load lasted weeks. Finance reconciliation lasted longer.
The incident timeline looked absurd in the doc:
→ Minute zero: auto-merge enabled for demo → Minute four: first duplicate detected by a customer email, not by alerts → Minute eleven: flag killed manually → Hour six: team agrees rollback is not a single button → Day three: production consistent again after manual repair scripts
The demo proved velocity. It also proved that velocity without human gates is borrowing against customer trust.
What We Changed After
Auto-merge is off indefinitely for anything touching money, identity, or irreversible migrations. That sounds obvious now. It was not obvious when the room wanted a viral internal clip.
Broader lessons:
→ Generated tests require independent oracles spelled out in the spec — compare against ledger rows, not fixture files → Migrations get human review even when code is agent-drafted → Feature flags default off in production unless a human explicitly flips the default in a separate PR → Demo modes run against isolated tenants, not shared production tables → Rollback drills happen before merge for tier-one paths, not after incidents
We also split “CI green” from “merge approved.” CI is necessary. It is not sufficient when the test suite was co-authored by the bug.
The Three-Day Rollback Reality
Forward-only migrations are a common agent suggestion because they look clean. Clean is not the same as reversible.
Our down migration existed on paper. It had never been run against a copy with realistic volume. Running it live would have dropped columns finance still needed for audit. We chose forward repair instead — slower, safer for compliance, miserable for sleep.
Repair scripts are where generated code hurts most. You cannot prompt your way out of duplicated rows without understanding which rows are canonical. That understanding lived in one engineer’s head and two Slack threads, not in the agent transcript.
Three days taught the org more than six months of green dashboards. Whether that lesson sticks depends on whether we keep treating CI color as moral judgment.
A Question for Your Team
If every test and implementation line in a PR came from the same prompt session, what independent evidence do you have that the change is safe?
If the answer is “another model reviewed it,” you have correlation, not independence.
Human review is not perfect. It is slower and sometimes catches the category error — the missing constraint, the wrong default, the test that proves the mock.
Eleven minutes is enough time to learn that lesson expensively. You can learn it cheaper in a tabletop exercise first.
Independence Is a Spec Item Now
After the incident we rewrote our spec template with a section called independent oracle. It must answer: how do we know this works without trusting the same session that built it?
Examples that survived legal and security review:
→ Compare webhook outcomes against payment provider ledger exports, not fixture JSON → Run migration against a restored production snapshot with anonymized data → Load test with concurrent clients in staging that mirrors worker network paths → Have a human who did not prompt the change execute the rollback script in staging
That section feels heavy for small tickets. Small tickets touching money are how big incidents start. Weight scales with blast radius, not with story points.
The Demo Culture Problem.
Internal demos reward spectacle. Auto-merge on green is spectacle. It compresses complexity into a clip that looks like the future. The future still includes finance, compliance, and customers who do not care that your CI bar was green.
We still demo agent workflows. We demo them on isolated tenants with synthetic money and a human narrating what would have gone wrong in shared production. The narration is the education. The green button is not.
Teaching Reviewers to Distrust Correlated Green.
Reviewers need permission to distrust correlated green. That permission comes from leaders who do not punish slow merges on tier-one paths.
Ask in review: what would fail if the payment provider retries twice? What happens if this flag is on by default? What row locks appear under peak load? Those questions are not anti-agent. They are pro-customer.
Three days of rollback taught our org more than a quarter of dashboard green. I wish we had learned cheaper. You still can.
Finance Was the Real Postmortem Audience.
Support cleaned up customer-facing damage. Finance cleaned up ledger damage. Engineering cleaned up code. The postmortem audience that mattered longest was finance because duplicated subscriptions touch recognition, refunds, and audit trails.
Technical root cause was a race. Organizational root cause was treating green CI as delegation of judgment. The second cause appears in every agent incident I have seen this year. The first cause varies. The second is consistent.
Document organizational root causes with the same rigor as technical ones or you will pay eleven-minute tuition repeatedly.
Playbooks for Tier-One Paths.
We wrote tier-one playbooks for changes that touch money, identity, migrations, and permission models. Playbooks include: required spec sections, required staging tenants, forbidden auto-merge flags, mandatory human rollback drill checklist.
Playbooks are not anti-speed. They are anti-surprise. Surprise scales poorly with customer trust.
If you are building auto-merge tooling, start with tier-three paths and earn the right to automate upward. We did not earn it. We assumed. Assumption was eleven minutes long. Consequences were three days deep.
What We Tell New Hires Now.
New hires hear about the eleven-minute window in onboarding now — not as legend, as policy context. They learn that green CI is a gate, not a guarantee. They learn that tier-one paths have playbooks before they touch billing code. That story takes ten minutes and prevents the confidence trap that hurt us.
If your org has a similar story and does not tell it, you are relying on luck to teach judgment. Luck scales worse than agents.
Customers Do Not Read CI Logs.
Customers experience outcomes. Duplicate charges. Missing exports. Locked accounts. They do not care that your pipeline was green. They care that you knew what merge meant before you clicked.
Keep that sentence visible in review culture. It is the antidote to demo brain.
What We Changed After the Rollback
We did not ban agents. We banned unsupervised merges for paths that touch money, auth, and migrations.
New rules that stuck:
→ Generated tests cannot be the only oracle for generated code on critical paths → Auto-merge requires a human-owned checklist item that names the blast radius → Any PR that adds both implementation and its primary tests from the same session gets a second reviewer who did not prompt either → Feature flags default to off for agent-authored behavior until a soak window passes
Eleven minutes taught us more than eleven slide decks. Green is a color. Safety is a process.
